1: wayhî bayyôm haššᵉlîšî wattilbaš ʾestēr malḵût: 'on the third day Esther clothed herself with royalty (malḵût).' The third day marks action after the three-day fast of Esther 4:16. Wattilbaš malḵût: literally 'she clothed herself with royalty/kingdom' — the word malḵût (kingship, royalty) rather than the usual bigdê malḵût (royal garments) may be intentional: she takes on the full identity and authority of queenship, not merely the costume. Waṯṯaʿămōḏ baḥăṣar bêt-hammelek happᵉnîmît: 'she stood in the inner court (ḥăṣar… happᵉnîmît) of the king's house' — the threshold whose law sentences her to death for unauthorized entry. She makes herself visible, positioning herself in the king's sight line before making any request. The entire approach is strategic: be seen before speaking, let the visual encounter establish favor before the verbal request is made.
2: wayhî kirʾôt hammelek ʾet-ʾestēr hammalḵāh ʿōmeḏet beḥāṣēr nāśᵉʾāh ḥēn bᵉʿênāyw: 'when the king saw Queen Esther standing in the court, she found favor (ḥēn) in his eyes.' Nāśᵉʾāh ḥēn bᵉʿênāyw: 'she bore/carried favor in his eyes' — the same unusual participial construction as Esther 2:15. The favor is presented as intrinsic to Esther rather than a royal decision: the king responds to something in her. Wayyôšeṭ hammelek lᵉʾestēr ʾet-šarḇiṭ hazzāhāḇ: 'the king extended the golden scepter (šarḇiṭ hazzāhāḇ) in his hand.' The scepter extension is the reprieve from the death sentence of Esther 4:11 — the moment of divine favor made tangible in royal gesture. Wattiqraḇ ʾestēr wattîgaʿ bᵉrōʾš haššarḇiṭ: 'and Esther approached and touched the top of the scepter' — the touch completes the acceptance of grace: favor offered (scepter extended) and received (scepter touched).
3: wayyōʾmer lāh hammelek mah-lāḵ ʾestēr hammalḵāh ûmah-baqqāšāṯēḵ ʿaḏ-ḥᵉṣî hammalḵût wᵉyinnāṭēn lāḵ: 'the king said, what is your petition, Queen Esther? It shall be granted to you, even to half the kingdom.' ʿAḏ-ḥᵉṣî hammalḵût (even to half the kingdom) is a standard ancient Near Eastern royal generosity formula — a hyperbolic offer of extreme favor (cf. Mark 6:23). It signals maximal readiness to grant whatever is within reason. The immediate generosity — before Esther speaks her petition — shows both the effectiveness of her approach (her clothed-in-royalty visual presentation) and the favorable reception the thirty-day absence had been building: the king missed her, and her appearing, having risked death to do so, demonstrates a devotion that moves him.
4: wattōʾmer ʾestēr ʾim-ʿal-hammelek ṭôḇ yāḇôʾ hammelek wᵉhāmān hayyôm ʾel-hammiště ʾăšer-ʿāśîtî lô: 'if it pleases the king, let the king and Haman come today to the banquet I have prepared.' Esther defers her petition to the banquet — the delay strategy. This has generated extensive commentary: she may hesitate because the moment is not right, because she wants to ensure Haman's presence before making the accusation, because she needs divine confirmation, or because the providential narrative requires one more night for the insomniac king's chronicles-reading (Esther 6). Whatever the human motivation, the invitation converts the emergency court appearance into a controlled social event where royal generosity is maximized and the setting is most favorable for a life-or-death petition.
5: wayyōʾmer hammelek maharû ʾet-hāmān laʿăśôt ʾet-dᵉḇar ʾestēr: 'the king said, bring Haman quickly so that what Esther desires may be done.' The king's enthusiasm — maharû (hurry! quickly!) — reflects his delight. Haman is summoned with the same urgency (dᵉḥûpîm, Esther 3:15) that sent the genocidal couriers out — the mirror of the plot: the haste that spread the death decree now spreads the dinner invitation to the death decree's author. Haman arrives at the banquet unaware that the queen's hospitality is the prelude to his accusation. The dinner invitation that honors him is simultaneously the summons to his judgment — the book's structural irony operating at full capacity.
6: wayyōʾmer hammelek lᵉʾestēr bᵉmištehhayyin mah-šᵉʾēlāṯēḵ wᵉyinnāṭēn lāḵ ûmah-baqqāšāṯēḵ ʿaḏ-ḥᵉṣî hammalḵût wᵉtēʿāś: 'at the wine banquet the king said, What is your petition? Up to half the kingdom it shall be done.' The wine banquet repetition of the throne room offer shows the king's continued generous posture. The setting — bᵉmištehhayyin (at the wine drinking) — mirrors the banquet of Esther 1 where wine played a destructive role (Vashti's humiliation). The book's banquet symmetry: what wine destroyed in Esther 1 (Vashti's queenship through an intemperate command), wine will enable in Esther 5-7 (Esther's victory through strategic hospitality). The mišteh institution frames both the problem and its resolution.
7: wattaʿan ʾestēr wattōʾmer šᵉʾēlātî ûḇaqqāšātî: 'Esther answered, My petition and my request —' The sentence deliberately trails off — Esther begins to name her petition and then pivots to a second invitation. The rhetorical technique of beginning the answer and then deferring has been analyzed as theatrical suspense, strategic preparation of emotional context, or providential delay. The same night Esther delays, the king cannot sleep and reads the chronicles, leading to Mordecai's honor (Esther 6) — the delay that appears as Esther's hesitation is the hinge on which the entire reversal turns. Whether Esther knows this or not is left open; the book's theology of hidden providence does not require that its human instruments understand the divine timing they are participating in.
8: ʾim-māṣāʾtî ḥēn bᵉʿênê hammelek wᵉʾim-ʿal-hammelek ṭôḇ lātēt ʾet-šᵉʾēlātî wᵉlaʿăśôt ʾet-baqqāšātî yāḇôʾ hammelek wᵉhāmān ʾel-hammiště ʾăšer ʾeʿěśeh lāhem: 'if I have found favor and if it pleases the king to grant my petition, let the king and Haman come to the banquet I will prepare.' Two conditional clauses of maximal deference before the actual request — Esther's language is calculated submission, not weakness. Ûḇᵉḵāl-ûᵉmāḥār ʾeʿăśeh kidᵉḇar hammelek: 'and tomorrow I will do as the king says' — she frames the second invitation as compliance with royal preference, giving the king what he wants (her hospitality) while she prepares what she needs (one more opportunity at the moment of maximum emotional readiness).
9: wayyēṣēʾ hāmān bayyôm hahûʾ śāmēaḥ wᵉṭôḇ lēḇ: 'Haman went out that day joyful and glad of heart.' Śāmēaḥ wᵉṭôḇ lēḇ (joyful and good of heart) is the idiom for festive elation — the same phrase used of the populace at Esther's coronation (Esther 2:18) and at the Purim celebration (Esther 8:15). Haman leaves the banquet at the apex of his public life. Ûkᵉrʾôt hāmān ʾet-morḏᵉḵay bᵉšaʿar hammelek wᵉlōʾ-qām wᵉlōʾ-zāʿ mimennû: 'but when Haman saw Mordecai at the king's gate, and Mordecai neither rose nor trembled before him.' Zāʿ (trembled) — the expected response from an inferior to a superior was physical prostration and fear. Mordecai does neither: he does not rise (social acknowledgment) and does not tremble (emotional deference). Wayyimmālēʾ hāmān ʿal-morḏᵉḵay ḥēmāh: 'Haman was filled with rage against Mordecai.' The verb mālaʾ (to be filled) with ḥēmāh (burning wrath) describes a man whose entire emotional capacity is occupied by a single offense — the man who just emerged from a royal banquet in his honor cannot enjoy that honor because one gate-official refuses to acknowledge him. The psychological portrait is precise: authoritarian power requires total submission, and total happiness is destroyed by a single exception.
10: wayyitʾappaq hāmān wayyāḇôʾ ʾel-bêtô wᵉyišlaḥ wayyāḇēʾ ʾet-ʾōhăḇāyw wᵉʾet-zereš ʾišṯô: 'Haman restrained himself (wayyitʾappaq) and went home, sending for his friends and his wife Zeresh.' Hitʾappeq (reflexive of ʾāpaḳ = to hold back) — to restrain oneself with effort; used of Joseph suppressing emotion (Genesis 43:31; Esther 45:1). Haman exercises a dangerous self-control: the rage is not released but compressed, making the subsequent explosion more calculated. Zereš is likely a Persian name — possibly Zarish or Zaharish, related to Old Persian zaranya (gold). The summons of 'friends and wife' mirrors the royal counsel structure of Esther 1-2: important decisions are made with an advisory circle. Haman reconstitutes his court in his home, with himself as king — the structural parallel that anticipates his downfall.
11: wayyᵉsappēr lāhem hāmān ʾet-kᵉḇôḏ ʿōšrô wᵉrōḇ bānāyw wᵉʾēt kol-ʾăšer giḏḏᵉlô hammelek wᵉʾēt ʾăšer nᵉśāʾô ʿal-haśśārîm wᵉʿaḇdê hammelek: 'Haman recounted to them the glory of his riches, the number of his sons, and everything the king had done to honor him.' Three categories of boast: kᵉḇôḏ ʿōšrô (glory of his wealth), rōḇ bānāyw (multitude of his sons — ten are named in Esther 9:7-10), and royal elevation above all princes. The threefold honor-catalogue is the standard ancient biography of a great man's achievement: wealth (economic power), sons (biological legacy), and royal status (political prestige). The sons are not incidental — in the ancient world many sons meant divine favor and household strength. But the exhaustive recitation of honors to his inner circle reveals the psychology: Haman requires constant external validation to maintain his sense of self, and no amount of honors is sufficient so long as one man withholds acknowledgment.
12: wayyōʾmer hāmān ʾap̄ lōʾ-hēḇîʾāh ʾestēr hammalḵāh ʿim-hammelek ʾel-hammiště ʾăšer-ʿāśāṯāh kî ʾim-ʾōṯî: 'Haman said, moreover, Queen Esther brought no one but me to the banquet she gave with the king.' ʾAp̄ (moreover, furthermore) introduces the crowning piece of the boast: even above wealth and sons, the exclusive dinner invitation. The honor is defined by exclusivity — 'none but me' with the king and queen. The verse's savage irony is invisible to Haman: the exclusive dinner that is his greatest boast is the setup for his accusation, disgrace, and execution. The gathering that he takes as proof of Esther's favor is the mechanism of his destruction — the honor that is simultaneously the death summons. The book's providential structure uses Haman's own pride to deliver him to his judgment.
13: wᵉḵol-zeh ʾênennû šōweh lî bᵉḵol-ʿēt ʾăšer ʾănî rōʾeh ʾet-morḏᵉḵay hayyᵉhûḏî yōšēḇ bᵉšaʿar hammelek: 'yet all this is worth nothing (ʾênennû šōweh lî) to me whenever I see Mordecai the Jew sitting at the king's gate.' ʾÊnennû šōweh lî (it is not worth it to me) — the phrase of radical devaluation: wealth, sons, royal honor, and the queen's exclusive attention — all of it is negated by one man's presence. The label hayyᵉhûḏî (the Jew) attached to Mordecai's name is significant: Haman does not say 'Mordecai who will not bow,' he says 'Mordecai the Jew' — the grievance has already become ethnic rather than personal. The real conflict is not about Mordecai's individual conduct but about the existence of a people with a loyalty that supersedes imperial authority. The chapter's theological center: ethnic identity as the real source of Haman's obsession.
14: wattōʾmer lô zereš ʾišṯô wᵉḵol-ʾōhăḇāyw yēʿāśeh-ʿēṣ gāḇōah ḥămiššîm ʾammāh: 'his wife Zeresh and all his friends said, let a gallows (ʿēṣ) be made, fifty cubits high.' ʿEṣ (wood, tree, gallows) — the word first appeared at 2:23 for the conspirators' execution. The same instrument now threatens Mordecai; it will ultimately hang Haman (Esther 7:9-10). The fifty-cubit height (approximately 22-23 meters) is conspicuously public — the gallows is designed to be visible throughout Susa as a monument to Haman's power and Mordecai's disgrace. The height's absurdity (nearly as tall as a modern five-story building) is characteristic of the book's comedy of imperial excess. Wattōʾmar lô zereš baqšē mimmenû hammelek lᵉhāmān wᵉhēṭaḇ liḇbᵉḵā: Zeresh urges Haman to request the execution from the king, and Haman's heart is good (wayyîṭaḇ lip̄nê hāmān haddāḇār) — the same ṭôḇ formula used for the king's royal approvals throughout Esther 1-3 here applied to Haman's pleasure in his wife's death-plan. The ʿēṣ thread that begins in Esther 2:23, intensifies here, and resolves in Esther 7:9-10 is the book's central gallows reversal: the instrument of death plotted against the innocent becomes the instrument of judgment against the guilty.
